message:
feat(ftui-core): improve inline mode safety and add validation helpers

Enhances the InlineConfig and InlineRenderer with better edge case
handling and clearer documentation.

InlineConfig improvements (crates/ftui-core/src/inline_mode.rs):
- ui_top_row(): Guarantee return value is at least 1 (valid ANSI row)
  to prevent issues when ui_height equals term_height
- log_bottom_row(): Document that 0 indicates no room for logs
- Add is_valid() method to check if config supports inline mode
  (requires ui_height > 0, ui_height < term_height, term_height > 1)

InlineRenderer improvements:
- Add cursor_saved field to track cursor save/restore state
- Improve doc comment for enter() to mention hybrid strategy

Render crate updates:
- grapheme_pool.rs: Additional pooling optimizations
- lib.rs: Export new functionality

Text crate updates:
- lib.rs: Extended text handling utilities

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-10i.12.1","title":"Write Operational Playbook (Chapter 17)","description":"# Write Operational Playbook (Chapter 17)

## Background
Chapter 17 exists to prevent the common failure mode of kernel projects: infinite refinement without a stable deliverable.

The playbook is part technical, part process:
- which PRs require which test gates
- how ADRs are written and frozen
- the recommended shipping order (avoid the widget trap)
- what "agent-ergonomic" means in concrete UX terms

## Deliverable
Create a durable doc (e.g. `docs/operational-playbook.md`) that captures:

### Merge-gate rules (from 17.1)
- Any change touching: inline mode cursor policy, presenter output sequencing, input parser, width measurement
  must include appropriate tests (unit/property/pty/snapshot) and must cite which invariants are preserved.

### ADR discipline (from 17.2)
- ADR template (Context / Decision / Alternatives / Consequences / Test Plan)
- Rules for when an ADR is required

### Minimal deliverables order (from 17.3)
1) Kernel (buffer/diff/presenter + terminal session + inline stability)
2) Runtime (update/view + simulator + snapshot)
3) Harness (reference app proving inline/log/UI)
4) Widgets (only harness essentials)
5) Extras (feature-gated)

### Agent-ergonomic checklist (from 17.4)
- Hello world harness in <200 LOC
- Tool output streaming is one function call
- Pinned bottom UI is one config option
- Temporary full-screen modal supported

### Immediate Next Steps checklist (from 17.5)
Convert the listed "first PRs" into checkboxes and link them to the relevant Beads tasks.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Doc exists with the sections above.
- [ ] Doc explicitly ties each rule to a reason (cursor safety, one-writer, determinism).
- [ ] Doc links to the relevant ADR issues and test-suite epics.

 {"id":"bd-10i.12.2","title":"Write Glossary (Appendix B)","description":"# Write Glossary (Appendix B)

## Background
The plan includes a short glossary of terms that recur throughout the kernel design. This needs to be in-repo so future contributors don't need the plan doc to understand terminology.

## Deliverable
Create `docs/glossary.md` containing (at minimum) the glossary table below, plus any additional terms introduced by implementation.

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Cell** | Single grid position (content + fg + bg + attrs) |
| **Buffer** | 2D array of Cells representing display state |
| **Frame** | Buffer + metadata for a render pass |
| **Diff** | Set of (x, y) positions that changed between buffers |
| **Presenter** | State-tracked ANSI emitter |
| **Grapheme** | User-perceived character (may be multiple codepoints) |
| **GraphemePool** | Interned storage for complex grapheme clusters |
| **LinkRegistry** | URL storage for OSC 8 hyperlinks |
| **Scissor** | Clipping rectangle for rendering |
| **SGR** | Select Graphic Rendition (style codes) |
| **OSC** | Operating System Command (escape sequence) |
| **CSI** | Control Sequence Introducer (`\x1b[`) |
| **DEC** | Digital Equipment Corporation (terminal standard) |
| **ZWJ** | Zero Width Joiner (connects graphemes into compound) |
| **Porter-Duff** | Compositing algebra for alpha blending |

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `docs/glossary.md` exists.
- [ ] Table is present and correct.
- [ ] Any additional ftui-specific terms (e.g., TerminalWriter, ScreenMode, UiAnchor) are added with concise definitions.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"docs","created_at":"2026-01-31T23:08:02.312315920Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-31T23:08:02.312315920Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-10i.12.2","depends_on_id":"bd-10i.12","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-31T23:08:02.312315920Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-10i.12.3","title":"Write Risk Register + Mitigation Links (Appendix C)","description":"# Write Risk Register + Mitigation Links (Appendix C)

## Background
The plan includes a small Risk Register to keep engineering effort focused on the failure modes that destroy trust in a terminal UI kernel (cursor corruption, escape injection, width bugs, capability mismatches, unsafe creep, byte bloat, interleaved writes).

We want this risk list in-repo so:
- mitigations stay attached to code and tests
- adding features doesn't silently reintroduce known risks

## Deliverable
Create `docs/risk-register.md` that includes the plan's risks and extends them with:
- the *actual* Beads issue IDs that mitigate the risk
- the *actual* test suites / scripts that enforce the mitigation

## Required risks (from plan)
1) Inline mode cursor corruption
- Mitigation: PTY tests + strict cursor policy + centralized writer API for logs/UI.

1b) ANSI / OSC injection via untrusted log content
- Mitigation: sanitize-by-default + explicit opt-in raw passthrough + adversarial PTY tests.

2) Unicode width bugs
- Mitigation: curated test corpus + snapshot tests + ASCII fast path only when proven.

3) Terminal capability mismatches
- Mitigation: conservative defaults + feature detection + robust fallbacks.

4) Unsafe creep
- Mitigation: forbid unsafe by default; isolate any perf tricks; require tests + benches.

5) Presenter byte bloat
- Mitigation: run grouping + style-run coalescing + output-length benchmarks in CI.

6) Interleaved stdout writes from user code
- Mitigation: one-writer rule; TerminalSession/TerminalWriter; routing/capture patterns.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `docs/risk-register.md` exists and contains the risks above.
- [ ] Each mitigation links to specific Beads issues and/or specific test suites (unit/property/pty/e2e).
- [ ] The risk register includes a short section "How to add a new risk" and "When to revisit".","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"docs","created_at":"2026-01-31T23:08:14.246182Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-31T23:08:14.246182Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-10i.12.3","depends_on_id":"bd-10i.12","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-31T23:08:14.246182Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

- we can quickly confirm completeness
- we can see the dependency structure at a glance

 {"id":"bd-29v","title":"Implement Viewport/LogViewer widget (agent harness essential)","description":"# Implement Viewport/LogViewer Widget (Agent Harness Essential)

## Background & Strategic Importance
The Viewport/LogViewer is THE ESSENTIAL widget for agent harness UIs (Plan 12.4). It displays streaming logs with scrollback while maintaining UI chrome. This is the primary use case for FrankenTUI - Claude Code / Codex-style agent harness displays require a log viewer that:
1. Accepts high-frequency log line additions without flicker
2. Maintains auto-scroll behavior for "follow" mode
3. Allows manual scrolling to inspect history
4. Respects memory bounds via circular buffer
5. Integrates with inline mode's scrollback preservation

## Why This Is P1, Not P2
The plan (Section 12.4) explicitly states: "Viewport/Log Viewer Widget" is an "agent harness essential". Without this widget, the entire agent harness use case is blocked.

---

## Technical Design

### Core Data Structure

```rust
use std::collections::VecDeque;
use tracing::{debug, trace, instrument};

/// A scrolling log viewer optimized for streaming append-only content.
///
/// # Design Rationale
/// - VecDeque for O(1) push/pop at both ends (circular buffer eviction)
/// - Separate scroll_offset from auto_scroll flag for manual override
/// - wrap_mode configurable per-instance for different use cases
/// - Stateful widget pattern for scroll state preservation across renders
pub struct LogViewer {
    /// Log lines stored as styled Text (supports colors, hyperlinks)
    lines: VecDeque<Text>,
    /// Maximum lines to retain (memory bound)
    max_lines: usize,
    /// Current scroll offset from bottom (0 = bottom)
    scroll_offset: usize,
    /// Auto-scroll enabled (re-engages when scrolled to bottom)
    auto_scroll: bool,
    /// Line wrapping mode
    wrap_mode: WrapMode,
    /// Default style for lines
    style: Style,
    /// Highlight style for selected/focused line
    highlight_style: Option<Style>,
}

#[derive(Clone, Copy, Debug, Default)]
pub enum WrapMode {
    /// No wrapping, truncate long lines
    #[default]
    NoWrap,
    /// Wrap at any character boundary
    CharWrap,
    /// Wrap at word boundaries (Unicode-aware)
    WordWrap,
}

/// Separate state for StatefulWidget pattern
pub struct LogViewerState {
    /// Viewport height from last render (for page up/down)
    last_viewport_height: u16,
    /// Total visible line count from last render
    last_visible_lines: usize,
    /// Selected line index (for copy/selection features)
```

### Public API

```rust
impl LogViewer {
    /// Create a new LogViewer with specified max line capacity.
    ///
    /// # Arguments
    /// * `max_lines` - Maximum lines to retain. When exceeded, oldest lines
    ///   are evicted. Recommend 10,000-100,000 for typical agent use cases.
    pub fn new(max_lines: usize) -> Self {
        debug!(max_lines, "Creating LogViewer");
        Self {
            lines: VecDeque::with_capacity(max_lines.min(1024)),
            max_lines,
            scroll_offset: 0,
            auto_scroll: true,
            wrap_mode: WrapMode::NoWrap,
            style: Style::default(),
            highlight_style: None,
        }
    }

    /// Append a single log line.
    ///
    /// # Performance
    /// - O(1) amortized for append
    /// - O(1) for eviction when at capacity
    ///
    /// # Auto-scroll Behavior
    /// If auto_scroll is enabled, view stays at bottom after push.
    #[instrument(skip(self, line), fields(line_count = self.lines.len()))]
    pub fn push(&mut self, line: impl Into<Text>) {
        let line = line.into();
        trace!("Pushing log line");

        // Evict oldest if at capacity
        if self.lines.len() >= self.max_lines {
            self.lines.pop_front();
            trace!("Evicted oldest line (at max_lines capacity)");
        }

        self.lines.push_back(line);

        // Auto-scroll: keep view at bottom
        if self.auto_scroll {
            self.scroll_offset = 0;
        }
    }

    /// Append multiple lines efficiently.
    pub fn push_many(&mut self, lines: impl IntoIterator<Item = impl Into<Text>>);

    /// Scroll up by N lines. Disables auto-scroll.
    pub fn scroll_up(&mut self, lines: usize);

    /// Scroll down by N lines. Re-enables auto-scroll if at bottom.
    pub fn scroll_down(&mut self, lines: usize);

    /// Jump to top of log history.
    pub fn scroll_to_top(&mut self);

    /// Jump to bottom and re-enable auto-scroll.
    pub fn scroll_to_bottom(&mut self);

    /// Page up (scroll by viewport height).
    pub fn page_up(&mut self, state: &LogViewerState);

    /// Page down (scroll by viewport height).
    pub fn page_down(&mut self, state: &LogViewerState);

    /// Check if currently scrolled to the bottom.
    pub fn is_at_bottom(&self) -> bool;

    /// Total line count in buffer.
    pub fn line_count(&self) -> usize;
}
```

### Rendering Implementation

```rust
impl StatefulWidget for LogViewer {
    type State = LogViewerState;

    #[instrument(skip(self, area, buf, state), fields(
        area.width = area.width,
        area.height = area.height,
        line_count = self.lines.len(),
        scroll_offset = self.scroll_offset
    ))]
    fn render(&self, area: Rect, buf: &mut Buffer, state: &mut Self::State) {
        // Update state with current viewport info
        state.last_viewport_height = area.height;

        // Calculate visible range (scroll_offset=0 means newest at bottom)
        let visible_count = area.height as usize;
        let total_lines = self.lines.len();
        let end_idx = total_lines.saturating_sub(self.scroll_offset);
        let start_idx = end_idx.saturating_sub(visible_count);

        trace!(start_idx, end_idx, visible_count, "Calculated visible range");

        // Render visible lines with wrap mode
        for (row_offset, line_idx) in (start_idx..end_idx).enumerate() {
            let y = area.y + row_offset as u16;
            let line = &self.lines[line_idx];
            self.render_line(line, area.x, y, area.width, buf);
        }

        // Render scroll indicator if not at bottom
        if self.scroll_offset > 0 && area.width >= 4 {
            let indicator = format!(" ↓{} ", self.scroll_offset);
            buf.set_string(area.x + area.width - indicator.len() as u16,
                area.y + area.height - 1, &indicator, Style::default().bg(Color::DarkGray));
        }
    }
}
```

## Background
The plan mentions Kitty keyboard protocol in TerminalCapabilities detection and ADR-004. This is an optional enhancement that provides richer key event information.

## What Kitty Keyboard Protocol Provides
- Distinguish key press vs repeat vs release
- Modifier key events (Shift pressed, Shift released)
- Proper handling of key combinations
- Unicode codepoint of key
- Alternate key representations

## Why It's Optional (P3)
- Only supported by Kitty, WezTerm, Ghostty, foot
- Traditional CSI sequences cover most use cases
- Additional complexity for marginal benefit
- Feature-gated to avoid bloating core

## Technical Design

### Detection
```rust
impl TerminalCapabilities {
    pub fn detect() -> Self {
        // ...
        let kitty_keyboard = term.contains("kitty") ||
            std::env::var("KITTY_WINDOW_ID").is_ok() ||
            // Could also detect WezTerm, Ghostty
            false;
    }
}
```

### Protocol Activation
```rust
impl TerminalSession {
    pub fn enable_kitty_keyboard(&mut self) -> io::Result<()> {
        // CSI > flags u
        // flags: 0b1111 = disambiguate + report event types + report alternate keys + report all keys
        self.writer.write_all(b"\x1b[>15u")?;
        self.kitty_keyboard_active = true;
        Ok(())
    }
    
    pub fn disable_kitty_keyboard(&mut self) -> io::Result<()> {
        // CSI < u - pop keyboard mode
        self.writer.write_all(b"\x1b[<u")?;
        self.kitty_keyboard_active = false;
        Ok(())
    }
}
```

### Enhanced Event Type
```rust
pub struct KeyEvent {
    pub code: KeyCode,
    pub modifiers: Modifiers,
    pub kind: KeyEventKind,  // Press, Repeat, Release
    // Kitty-only extensions:
    pub base_codepoint: Option<char>,
    pub shifted_key: Option<char>,
}

pub enum KeyEventKind {
    Press,   // Key pressed
    Repeat,  // Key held down (auto-repeat)
    Release, // Key released
}
```

### Parser Extension
```rust
impl InputParser {
    fn parse_kitty_key(&mut self, seq: &[u8]) -> Option<Event> {
        // CSI unicode-codepoint ; modifiers ; event-type u
        // Parse the enhanced sequence
        // ...
    }
}
```

## Graceful Degradation
- If Kitty protocol not available, fall back to standard CSI
- KeyEventKind defaults to Press
- Optional fields are None for legacy input

## Tests Required
- [ ] Detection works for Kitty terminal
- [ ] Protocol activation/deactivation
- [ ] Parse enhanced key sequences
- [ ] Modifier key press/release events
- [ ] Graceful fallback when not supported
- [ ] RAII cleanup on exit

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Feature-gated behind `kitty-keyboard` feature
- [ ] Detection in TerminalCapabilities
- [ ] Activation/deactivation in TerminalSession
- [ ] Enhanced KeyEvent fields
- [ ] Parser handles Kitty sequences
- [ ] Falls back gracefully
- [ ] Documented in compatibility matrix

## Background & Motivation

Clipboard access varies across terminals and platforms:
- OSC 52 (modern terminals)
- pbcopy/pbpaste (macOS)
- xclip/xsel (X11)
- wl-copy/wl-paste (Wayland)

**The solution:**
- Unified Clipboard API
- Auto-detect best method
- Graceful fallback chain

**Relationship to existing beads:**
- Requires bd-10i.4.4 (TerminalCapabilities detection)
- Extends bd-2rs (OSC 52 implementation)

## Technical Design

```rust
pub struct Clipboard {
    backend: ClipboardBackend,
}

pub enum ClipboardBackend {
    Osc52,
    MacOS,  // pbcopy/pbpaste
    X11,    // xclip
    Wayland, // wl-copy
    Unavailable,
}

pub enum ClipboardError {
    NotAvailable,
    WriteError(String),
    ReadError(String),
}

impl Clipboard {
    pub fn detect(caps: &TerminalCapabilities) -> Self {
        // Try OSC 52 first if terminal supports it
        // Fall back to platform-specific tools
    }
    
    pub fn set(&self, text: &str) -> Result<(), ClipboardError> { ... }
    pub fn get(&self) -> Result<String, ClipboardError> { ... }
}
```

## Logging Requirements

```rust
// Backend detected (INFO level)
tracing::info!(backend = ?backend, "Clipboard backend detected");

// Clipboard write (DEBUG level)
tracing::debug!(backend = ?backend, bytes = text.len(), "Clipboard write");

// Fallback triggered (WARN level)
tracing::warn!(
    primary = ?primary_backend,
    fallback = ?fallback_backend,
    reason = reason,
    "Clipboard fallback triggered"
);
```

## E2E Tests

```bash
# Test clipboard roundtrip
./ftui-harness --test-mode clipboard --text "Hello, World!"
# Assert: get() returns same text

# Test with large content
./ftui-harness --test-mode clipboard --size 100KB
# Assert: content preserved

# Test fallback chain
FTUI_CLIPBOARD_BACKEND=none ./ftui-harness --test-mode clipboard --expect-fallback
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] OSC 52 implementation works in modern terminals
- [ ] Platform fallback (macOS, Linux X11, Linux Wayland, Windows)
- [ ] Auto-detection of best method
- [ ] Clear error messages for unsupported environments
- [ ] Works in SSH sessions (OSC 52 preferred)
- [ ] Binary content base64 encoded
- [ ] All tests pass","notes":"## Important: Timeout Handling for OSC 52

OSC 52 clipboard read requires careful timeout handling:

```rust
impl Clipboard {
    /// Read from clipboard with timeout
    /// OSC 52 responses may not arrive or be slow
    pub fn get_with_timeout(&self, timeout: Duration) -> Result<String, ClipboardError> {
        match self.backend {
            ClipboardBackend::Osc52 => {
                // Send OSC 52 query
                self.send_osc52_query()?;
                
                // Wait for response with timeout
                match self.wait_for_response(timeout) {
                    Ok(data) => Ok(base64::decode(&data)?),
                    Err(TimeoutError) => {
                        // Fall back to platform clipboard
                        self.fallback_get()
                    }
                }
            }
            _ => self.platform_get(),
        }
    }
}
```

Default timeout: 100ms (configurable)

Also add Timeout variant to error enum:
```rust
pub enum ClipboardError {
    NotAvailable,
    WriteError(String),
    ReadError(String),
    Timeout,  // OSC 52 response didn't arrive
}
```

-{"id":"bd-2rs","title":"Implement clipboard integration (OSC 52)","description":"PHASE 7 EXTRAS - CLIPBOARD INTEGRATION

## Background
OSC 52 allows setting the system clipboard from terminal applications. This enables copy/paste functionality without external dependencies.

## Technical Requirements

### OSC 52 Protocol
Sequence: ESC ] 52 ; c ; <base64-data> BEL

c = clipboard selection:
- c = clipboard
- p = primary (X11)
- s = secondary
- 0-7 = cut buffers

### Clipboard API

pub struct Clipboard {
    caps: TerminalCapabilities,
}

impl Clipboard {
    /// Check if OSC 52 is supported
    pub fn is_available(&self) -> bool {
        self.caps.osc52
    }
    
    /// Set clipboard content
    pub fn set(&self, content: &str, writer: &mut impl Write) -> io::Result<()> {
        if !self.is_available() {
            return Err(io::Error::new(
                io::ErrorKind::Unsupported,
                "OSC 52 not available"
            ));
        }
        
        let encoded = base64::encode(content);
        write!(writer, "\x1b]52;c;{}\x07", encoded)?;
        writer.flush()
    }
    
    /// Clear clipboard
    pub fn clear(&self, writer: &mut impl Write) -> io::Result<()> {
        write!(writer, "\x1b]52;c;\x07")?;
        writer.flush()
    }
}

### Terminal Compatibility
OSC 52 support varies:
- ✅ iTerm2, kitty, alacritty, WezTerm, foot
- ⚠️ tmux (needs set-clipboard on)
- ⚠️ screen (needs clipboard support)
- ❌ Many terminal emulators disable by default

### Detection Strategy
1. Check known terminal from TERM/TERM_PROGRAM
2. Check tmux/screen allow-passthrough
3. Attempt set and check for response (risky)
4. Default: assume unsupported

### Size Limits
Most terminals limit OSC 52 payload:
- Common limit: 74994 bytes (base64)
- Split large content or fail gracefully

### Fallback Options
When OSC 52 unavailable:
- Fall back to external clipboard tool (feature: clipboard-fallback)
  - macOS: pbcopy/pbpaste
  - Linux: xclip, xsel, wl-copy
  - Windows: clip.exe
- Or return error for application to handle

## Tests Required
- [ ] OSC 52 sequence generation
- [ ] Base64 encoding correct
- [ ] Size limit handling
- [ ] Clear clipboard works
- [ ] Capability detection
- [ ] Fallback to external tools
- [ ] Error handling when unavailable

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] OSC 52 set clipboard
- [ ] Capability detection
- [ ] Size limit enforcement
- [ ] Clear clipboard
- [ ] Optional external fallback
- [ ] Feature gate: clipboard

 {"id":"bd-2tyd","title":"Implement CachedWidget<W> wrapper","description":"# Implement CachedWidget<W> wrapper

## Background
Part of Frame Caching (bd-6qxh). Cache rendered widget output to avoid re-rendering static content.

## Technical Design

```rust
/// Widget wrapper that caches rendered output
pub struct CachedWidget<W: Widget> {
    /// Inner widget
    inner: W,
    /// Cached buffer
    cache: Option<CachedBuffer>,
    /// Last rendered area
    last_area: Option<Rect>,
    /// Dirty flag (needs re-render)
    dirty: bool,
    /// Cache key (for invalidation)
    cache_key: u64,
}

/// Cached buffer with metadata
struct CachedBuffer {
    buffer: Buffer,
    hash: u64,
    rendered_at: Instant,
}

impl<W: Widget> CachedWidget<W> {
    pub fn new(inner: W) -> Self {
        Self {
            inner,
            cache: None,
            last_area: None,
            dirty: true,
            cache_key: 0,
        }
    }
    
    /// Mark cache as dirty (needs re-render)
    pub fn mark_dirty(&mut self) {
        self.dirty = true;
    }
    
    /// Invalidate if model hash changed
    pub fn invalidate_if_changed(&mut self, new_hash: u64) {
        if self.cache_key \!= new_hash {
            self.dirty = true;
            self.cache_key = new_hash;
        }
    }
    
    /// Get inner widget
    pub fn inner(&self) -> &W {
        &self.inner
    }
    
    /// Get mutable inner widget (marks dirty)
    pub fn inner_mut(&mut self) -> &mut W {
        self.dirty = true;
        &mut self.inner
    }
}

impl<W: Widget> Widget for CachedWidget<W> {
    fn render(&mut self, area: Rect, buf: &mut Buffer, budget: &RenderBudget) {
        // Check if we need to re-render
        let needs_render = self.dirty
            || self.last_area \!= Some(area)
            || self.cache.is_none();
        
        if needs_render {
            // Render to cache buffer
            let mut cache_buf = Buffer::empty(area);
            self.inner.render(area, &mut cache_buf, budget);
            
            self.cache = Some(CachedBuffer {
                buffer: cache_buf,
                hash: self.cache_key,
                rendered_at: Instant::now(),
            });
            self.last_area = Some(area);
            self.dirty = false;
            
            tracing::debug\!(
                widget = std::any::type_name::<W>(),
                "Cache miss - re-rendered"
            );
        } else {
            tracing::trace\!(
                widget = std::any::type_name::<W>(),
                "Cache hit"
            );
        }
        
        // Composite cache to output
        if let Some(cache) = &self.cache {
            buf.merge(&cache.buffer);
        }
    }
    
    fn is_essential(&self) -> bool {
        self.inner.is_essential()
    }
}
```

## When to Use
- Static headers/footers
- Help text
- Fixed decorations
- Any widget that changes infrequently

## Background
Snapshot testing captures expected Buffer output and compares against future runs. Essential for preventing visual regressions and validating rendering correctness.

## Technical Requirements
- Snapshot capture:
  - Buffer → text representation (with ANSI or stripped)
  - Buffer → binary serialization
  - Configurable normalization (strip cursor position, etc.)
- Snapshot storage:
  - tests/snapshots/ directory
  - Filename based on test name + variant
  - Human-readable format for review
- Comparison:
  - Exact match mode
  - Fuzzy match mode (ignore whitespace variations)
  - Diff output on failure showing changes
- Update workflow:
  - BLESS=1 env var to update snapshots
  - cargo insta integration optional

## Design Rationale
Visual output is hard to assert with unit tests. Snapshots capture 'known good' output and flag any changes for human review. Prevents subtle rendering regressions.

 {"id":"bd-3kou","title":"Implement CompressedFrame with delta encoding","description":"# Implement CompressedFrame with delta encoding

## Background
Part of Time-Travel Debugging (bd-2lwf). Frames are stored as deltas from previous frame to minimize memory usage.

## Technical Design

```rust
/// Compressed frame (delta-encoded from previous)
pub struct CompressedFrame {
    /// Frame number
    frame_number: u64,
    /// Base frame number (None = full frame)
    base: Option<u64>,
    /// Changed cells only
    changes: Vec<CellChange>,
    /// Checksum for integrity
    checksum: u32,
    /// Size of original frame
    original_size: (u16, u16),
}

/// Single cell change
#[derive(Clone, Debug)]
pub struct CellChange {
    pub x: u16,
    pub y: u16,
    pub cell: Cell,
}

impl CompressedFrame {
    /// Create full frame snapshot (no base)
    pub fn full(frame: &Frame, frame_number: u64) -> Self {
        let mut changes = Vec::new();
        for y in 0..frame.height() {
            for x in 0..frame.width() {
                let cell = frame.get(x, y);
                if !cell.is_empty() {
                    changes.push(CellChange { x, y, cell: *cell });
                }
            }
        }
        
        Self {
            frame_number,
            base: None,
            changes,
            checksum: frame.checksum(),
            original_size: (frame.width(), frame.height()),
        }
    }
    
    /// Create delta from previous frame
    pub fn delta(current: &Frame, previous: &Frame, frame_number: u64, base_number: u64) -> Self {
        let mut changes = Vec::new();
        
        for y in 0..current.height() {
            for x in 0..current.width() {
                let curr_cell = current.get(x, y);
                let prev_cell = previous.get(x, y);
                
                if !curr_cell.bits_eq(prev_cell) {
                    changes.push(CellChange { x, y, cell: *curr_cell });
                }
            }
        }
        
        Self {
            frame_number,
            base: Some(base_number),
            changes,
            checksum: current.checksum(),
            original_size: (current.width(), current.height()),
        }
    }
    
    /// Apply changes to reconstruct frame
    pub fn apply_to(&self, frame: &mut Frame) {
        // Resize if needed
        if frame.size() != self.original_size {
            frame.resize(self.original_size.0, self.original_size.1);
        }
        
        for change in &self.changes {
            frame.set(change.x, change.y, change.cell);
        }
    }
    
    /// Memory size estimate
    pub fn memory_size(&self) -> usize {
        std::mem::size_of::<Self>() +
        self.changes.len() * std::mem::size_of::<CellChange>()
    }
    
    /// Number of changed cells
    pub fn change_count(&self) -> usize {
        self.changes.len()
    }
    
    /// Compression ratio
    pub fn compression_ratio(&self) -> f32 {
        let full_size = self.original_size.0 as usize * self.original_size.1 as usize * 16;
        self.memory_size() as f32 / full_size as f32
    }
}
```

## Typical Compression
- 5% cells changed per frame = ~5% storage
- Full frame every N frames for seeking
- Average: ~100 bytes per frame

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] CompressedFrame with full and delta modes

 {"id":"bd-3ky","title":"OpenTUI Core Systems (opentui_rust port)","description":"# OpenTUI Core Systems (reference extraction, not a blind port)

## Purpose
This epic exists for **traceability** to `opentui_rust`.

We are not doing a 1:1 port. Instead, we selectively extract ideas and components that strengthen FrankenTUI’s kernel or optional extras.

## Plan Alignment
The plan explicitly says:
- keep only kernel-strengthening primitives
- de-scope “the widget zoo” into feature-gated layers

This epic is a place to record what we *borrowed*, why, and where it lands in the ftui crate layout.

## Reference Source
- `legacy_reference_library_code/opentui_rust/src/`

## What We Intend To Reuse (High-level)
- Efficient terminal control sequence encoding (CSI/OSC/DEC modes)
- Buffer drawing helpers (lines/rects/borders) to implement widgets ergonomically
- Robust raw-mode / cleanup discipline patterns
- Optional text infrastructure (rope/editor/view) if/when we ship a richer editor
- Optional unicode utilities (normalization/search/bidi) if/when needed
- Optional tokenization infrastructure for syntax highlighting

## Tracked Issues (live elsewhere in the plan tree)
These issues are the concrete “extractions” and/or feature-gated extras derived from OpenTUI:
- `bd-3ky.1` ANSI sequence encoding helpers (feeds Presenter / TerminalWriter)
- `bd-3ky.2` Buffer drawing operations (feeds widget implementations)
- `bd-3ky.10` Raw mode handling + cleanup patterns (feeds TerminalSession / backend)
- `bd-3ky.3` Tokenizer engine (optional; feeds syntax highlighting)
- `bd-3ky.13` Language tokenizers (optional; feeds syntax highlighting)
- `bd-3ky.4` Highlight theme system (optional; feeds syntax highlighting)
- `bd-3ky.5` Rope (optional; feeds TextArea/editor)
- `bd-3ky.6` Editor core (optional; feeds TextArea/editor)
- `bd-3ky.14` Text View (optional; feeds Viewport/TextArea)
- `bd-3ky.7` BiDi (optional)
- `bd-3ky.8` Normalization (optional)
- `bd-3ky.9` Search (optional)

## Non-Goals
- A monolithic “OpenTUI port” that blocks ftui kernel shipping
- Shipping optional features before Gate 1–2 (inline stability + presenter correctness)

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Each tracked issue above is explicitly mapped to an ftui crate/module and feature flag (where applicable).
- [ ] Kernel-critical extractions (ANSI encoding, drawing ops, raw-mode discipline) have tests and are used by core ftui paths.
- [ ] Optional extras remain feature-gated and cannot destabilize inline mode or presenter invariants.
- [ ] This epic remains a traceability index (no duplicated implementation work hidden here).","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-01-31T23:00:44.420755776Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-01T00:23:27.294316159Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-3ky","depends_on_id":"bd-10i","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-31T23:57:58.959386119Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-3ky","depends_on_id":"bd-10i.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-31T23:04:26.303601247Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":4,"issue_id":"bd-3ky","author":"ubuntu","text":"Reopened: User requested reopening; reverting prior closure.","created_at":"2026-01-31T23:34:20Z"}]}
-{"id":"bd-3ky.1","title":"Implement ANSI sequence generator module","description":"# Implement ANSI sequence encoding helpers (CSI/OSC/DEC)

## Objective
Implement a small, well-tested set of helpers to encode the ANSI/VT control sequences that ftui emits.

This is **not** about terminal state tracking or diffing; it is only about producing correct bytes for known operations.

## Why This Exists
Presenter correctness depends on:
- emitting syntactically valid sequences
- emitting correctly terminated OSC strings
- using consistent conventions (1-indexed CUP, correct separators)

Isolating sequence encoding makes the Presenter easier to reason about and test.

## Reference (for ideas / completeness)
- `legacy_reference_library_code/opentui_rust/src/ansi/output.rs`
- `legacy_reference_library_code/opentui_rust/src/ansi/sequences.rs`

## Scope
Support encoding helpers for sequences we plan to use:

### CSI sequences
- SGR (reset, attributes, fg/bg: 16/256/truecolor)
- CUP cursor positioning (row/col, 1-indexed)
- relative cursor moves (optional; only if Presenter uses them)
- EL/ED erase variants used by Presenter
- DECSTBM scroll region (for inline mode optimizations)
- DECSET/DECRST toggles used by TerminalSession/TerminalWriter
- DEC 2026 synchronized output begin/end

### OSC sequences
- OSC 8 hyperlinks (start/end)
- (Optional) OSC 52 clipboard (Phase 7)
- (Optional) OSC title

### Modes
- bracketed paste enable/disable
- mouse tracking enable/disable (SGR encoding)

## Design Notes
- Keep this layer **pure**: it should generate bytes into a buffer, not own terminal state.
- Avoid per-call allocations where possible (reuse a scratch buffer).
- Prefer explicit, readable helpers over “clever” formatting.

## Integration Points
- `bd-10i.4.3` (Presenter) depends on this issue.
- TerminalSession / TerminalWriter may also reuse the same helpers for mode toggles.

## Tests Required
- Unit tests for exact bytes for each helper (table-driven).
- Unit tests for OSC termination correctness (ST vs BEL if supported).
- Property tests:
  - sequences are ASCII bytes
  - OSC sequences are always terminated

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Helper API exists for the sequences listed in Scope (or an explicit list of omissions is documented).
- [ ] Unit tests cover every helper and verify exact byte output.
- [ ] OSC 8 link sequences are always properly terminated.
- [ ] Presenter uses this layer (no ad-hoc string formatting for core sequences).","acceptance_criteria":"## Acceptance Criteria

 {"id":"bd-3ky.10","title":"Implement terminal raw mode handling","description":"# Implement terminal raw mode handling + cleanup discipline

## Objective
Implement the terminal backend glue needed for ftui to:
- enter raw mode
- enable/disable terminal modes (mouse, bracketed paste, focus, sync output)
- reliably restore the user’s terminal on exit and on panic

## Why This Matters
Most “TUI flakiness” is actually cleanup failure:
- cursor stays hidden
- raw mode persists
- bracketed paste remains enabled
- scroll region not reset

We must treat cleanup as a first-class invariant.

## Reference (for patterns)
- `legacy_reference_library_code/opentui_rust/src/` terminal/raw mode modules

## Scope
Implement (or wrap) backend operations:
- enter/exit raw mode (RAII)
- show/hide cursor
- enter/exit alt-screen (optional; inline mode is default)
- enable/disable:
  - bracketed paste
  - mouse (SGR)
  - focus events
  - sync output (DEC 2026) where supported
- ensure drop/cleanup executes even when unwinding (panic path)

## Design Notes
- This must integrate with the chosen backend (ADR-003).
- Cleanup must be idempotent (safe to call twice).
- Failures during cleanup should be logged but must not panic.

## Tests Required
- PTY integration tests (release mode where relevant) that assert after process exit:
  - raw mode is restored
  - cursor is visible
  - bracketed paste is disabled
  - mouse mode is disabled
  - alt-screen exited (if used)
  - scroll region reset (if used)

Scenarios:
- normal exit
- panic during render
- I/O error mid-write (best-effort cleanup)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] RAII guard exists and is used by runtime/harness entrypoints.
- [ ] Cleanup is idempotent and does not panic.
- [ ] PTY tests cover normal + panic cleanup and pass reliably.
- [ ] Mode toggles are routed through the one-writer policy (no ad-hoc stdout writes).","acceptance_criteria":"## Acceptance Criteria

### Functional Requirements
- [ ] Enter raw mode (disable line buffering, echo, signals)
- [ ] Exit raw mode (restore original termios)
- [ ] RAII guard for automatic cleanup on drop
- [ ] Panic hook for emergency cleanup on panic
- [ ] Alternative screen buffer enter/exit
- [ ] Mouse mode enable/disable
- [ ] Bracketed paste mode enable/disable
- [ ] Cursor visibility control
- [ ] Signal handling: SIGWINCH, SIGTERM, SIGINT

### Safety Requirements
- [ ] Terminal always restored on normal exit
- [ ] Terminal always restored on panic
- [ ] Terminal restored even on SIGKILL (best effort)
- [ ] No undefined behavior on double-enter or double-exit
- [ ] Thread-safe (only one raw mode active at a time)

### Platform Requirements
- [ ] Unix: termios manipulation
- [ ] macOS: termios with BSD extensions
- [ ] Windows: ConsoleMode manipulation (future)

### Logging Requirements
- [ ] tracing::info! on mode enter/exit
- [ ] tracing::debug! for termios changes
- [ ] tracing::error! for restoration failures
- [ ] tracing::warn! for signal handler issues

### Test Coverage
- [ ] Unit test: enter/exit cycle
- [ ] Unit test: RAII cleanup
- [ ] PTY test: isolation from parent terminal
- [ ] PTY test: cleanup after simulated panic
- [ ] Integration test: signal handling","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-31T23:02:12.911475071Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-01T00:25:27.966378772Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-3ky.10","depends_on_id":"bd-10i.10.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-01T00:25:14.984135057Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-3ky.10","depends_on_id":"bd-10i.2.6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-01T00:25:15.625294185Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-3ky.10","depends_on_id":"bd-10i.8","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-31T23:59:47.946966344Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":2,"issue_id":"bd-3ky.10","author":"ubuntu","text":"Reopened: User requested reopening; reverting prior closure.","created_at":"2026-01-31T23:34:20Z"}]}
 {"id":"bd-3ky.11","title":"Optional: runtime terminal capability probing (bounded timeouts)","description":"# Optional: runtime terminal capability probing (bounded timeouts)

## Background
Plan Section 0.4/9.1 + TerminalCapabilities contract: v1 detection should be conservative and environment-based.

However, the plan allows an optional *feature-gated* probing layer:
- device attribute queries (DA)
- OSC queries

Critical constraints:
- probing must be bounded with timeouts
- probing must fail-open (unknown => disable feature, never hang startup)
- probing must not compromise the one-writer rule

## Scope
Add an optional probing module that can refine `TerminalCapabilities`.

Examples (only if safe and supported):
- cursor position report (CPR)
- DA1/DA2 (device attributes)
- synchronized output support probing (if an unambiguous probe exists)
- background color query (for dark/light heuristics) if we decide to support it

## Design constraints
- Must run only when we have exclusive terminal ownership (TerminalSession active).
- Must be guarded by a feature flag (e.g., `caps-probe`).
- Must never block indefinitely; hard timeouts.

## Tests
- Unit tests for response parsing
- Integration tests (PTY) to ensure:
  - probe timeouts do not hang
  - capability results are conservative when probe fails

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Feature-gated probe module exists.
- [ ] All probes are bounded by timeouts and fail-open.
- [ ] Tests cover success + timeout + malformed responses.","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-31T23:02:19.243199848Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-31T23:18:00.759446498Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-3ky.11","depends_on_id":"bd-10i.2.6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-31T23:17:22.209661254Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-3ky.11","depends_on_id":"bd-10i.4","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-31T23:17:06.856234469Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-3ky.11","depends_on_id":"bd-10i.4.4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-31T23:17:22.796892855Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-3ky.12","title":"Implement optional render/output thread (Mode B)","description":"# Implement optional render/output thread (Mode B)

## Background
Plan Sections 0.9.2 (Mode B) and 8.2 describe an optional dedicated render/output thread.

This exists to solve the real harness problem:
- logs can be produced by background tasks / subprocess capture
- UI needs tick-driven updates
- if output interleaves, inline mode breaks

Mode B keeps the **one-writer rule** by construction: the render thread is the only place bytes hit the terminal.

## Scope
- Feature-gated "Mode B" operation in addition to the default single-threaded mode.
- Render/output thread owns:
  - `TerminalSession` output handle
  - `Presenter`
  - `prev_buffer` (front buffer)
  - inline-mode policy state (ui rect/anchor/height)

## Design
### Messages
```rust
enum OutMsg {
    Log(Vec<u8>),
    Render(Buffer),
    Resize { w: u16, h: u16 },
    SetMode(ScreenMode),
    Shutdown,
}
```

### Coalescing rules
- Render messages are coalesced ("latest wins"):
  - drop intermediate renders if a newer one arrives
  - present at most one buffer per loop iteration
- Log messages are *not* dropped, but are:
  - newline-aware
  - chunked to avoid starving UI

### Error handling
- Render thread errors propagate back to the Program so we can:
  - restore terminal state
  - exit with a useful error

## Test Plan
- PTY tests must run in both modes:
  - default single-threaded mode
  - render/output thread enabled
- Stress tests:
  - heavy log spam + UI tick (spinner) + resize storms
  - ensure cursor invariants and scrollback preservation

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Mode B can be enabled via feature flag and/or App/Program option.
- [ ] All terminal writes are serialized in the render thread.
- [ ] Render coalescing works (latest buffer presented).
- [ ] Log chunking works and does not starve UI.
- [ ] PTY tests pass in both Mode A and Mode B.

## Location
Likely `ftui-core` (thread + terminal ownership) with integration hooks in `ftui-runtime`.","acceptance_criteria":"## Acceptance Criteria

### Functional Requirements
- [ ] Background thread for frame rendering
- [ ] Double buffering with atomic swap
- [ ] Frame rate limiting (configurable FPS cap)
- [ ] Frame skipping when behind schedule
- [ ] Graceful shutdown without hanging
- [ ] Error propagation to main thread
- [ ] Submit frame (non-blocking)
- [ ] Flush pending frames (blocking)

### Threading Requirements
- [ ] Main thread never blocked on I/O
- [ ] Channel-based command passing
- [ ] No data races on buffer swap
- [ ] Proper synchronization on shutdown

### Performance Requirements
- [ ] Submit latency: < 100µs
- [ ] Render latency: < 1ms for 80x24
- [ ] Memory: 2 buffers + channel overhead
- [ ] No allocation per frame (buffer reuse)

### Error Handling
- [ ] I/O errors propagated to main thread
- [ ] Panics in render thread caught
- [ ] Graceful degradation on slow I/O

### Logging Requirements
- [ ] tracing::debug! for frame submission
- [ ] tracing::debug! for frame skip
- [ ] tracing::trace! for render timing
- [ ] tracing::error! for I/O failures
- [ ] tracing::info! for shutdown

- [ ] Unit test: frame rate limiting
- [ ] Stress test: high FPS submission
- [ ] Stress test: large frame buffers

+//! Text handling for FrankenTUI.
+//!
+//! This crate provides text primitives for styled text rendering:
+//! - [`Segment`] - atomic unit of styled text with cell-aware splitting
+//! - [`SegmentLine`] - a line of segments
+//! - [`SegmentLines`] - multi-line text
+//!
+//! # Example
+//! ```
+//! use ftui_text::{Segment, SegmentLine, split_into_lines};
+//! use ftui_style::Style;
+//!
+//! // Create styled segments
+//! let seg = Segment::styled("Error:", Style::new().bold());
+//!
+//! // Split text at cell boundaries
+//! let (left, right) = Segment::text("hello world").split_at_cell(5);
+//! assert_eq!(left.as_str(), "hello");
+//!
+//! // Handle multi-line text
+//! let lines = split_into_lines(vec![
+//!     Segment::text("line 1"),
+//!     Segment::newline(),
+//!     Segment::text("line 2"),
+//! ]);
+//! assert_eq!(lines.len(), 2);
+//! ```
+
+pub mod segment;
+
+pub use segment::{ControlCode, Segment, SegmentLine, SegmentLines, join_lines, split_into_lines};
